actions.thought: Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak starting hand, even three-handed on the Button. Folding is the only reasonable play here to preserve my stack.

Jd 7c is a weak offsuit hand to play from the small blind. Since the button has folded, I am only up against the big blind. I prefer to play tight in the opening hand of the tournament rather than investing more chips with a marginal hand out of position. Folding is the most disciplined choice here.

Ace-seven suited is a strong hand in the button position. I want to take the initiative and potentially steal the blinds or play a pot in position. Raising to 250 is a standard open-raise size to build the pot and exert pressure, which is well above the minimum raise requirement.

I am in the Small Blind with 3c 5h, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Flashlight) has opened with a raise to 2.5x. Calling out of position with a marginal holding like 3-5 offsuit is not profitable in the long run, and raising is too risky as a bluff this early. Folding is the most prudent action.
43o is an extremely weak starting hand. Even against a button raise where ranges might be wider, playing 43o out of position is highly unprofitable. Folding is the clear decision here.

I am on the button with 3h 2h. While this hand is suited and has some connectivity, it is extremely weak and easily dominated. In a 3-handed game, I should play a wider range from the button, but 3-2 suited is still at the very bottom of that range. Given we are early in the 150-hand match, I will choose to fold and wait for a more profitable spot rather than trying to steal or play a marginal hand out of position post-flop if called.

I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak hand, J3o, and the Button has folded. Against the Big Blind, it's generally best to play a tighter range, and J3o is easily a fold. No need to commit more chips with marginal holdings.

I am the Button, which is first to act pre-flop in a 3-handed game. I hold Q3s. While it is suited, this hand is too weak to open raise, especially with 100 BB effective stacks. It's best to fold and wait for better starting hands.

With 6d-Jc in the Small Blind and the Button having already folded, I am facing the Big Blind. This hand is weak and plays poorly out of position. It is standard to fold speculative, unsuited cards like J6o to preserve my stack rather than committing more chips to a potentially marginal post-flop situation.

I have been dealt 2d 3c, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in No-Limit Texas Hold'em. Regardless of my position as the button, there is no strategic value in investing chips with such a poor hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Folded to me in the Small Blind. Jack-Ten offsuit is a strong hand in three-handed play, especially when heads-up against the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and pressure the Big Blind, as this hand is well above the average holding.

I am in the Big Blind with Seven-Four offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand. Facing a raise to 300 from the Small Blind, the correct and mathematically sound decision is to fold, as defending with such a poor hand would be highly unprofitable.

K8o is a standard opening hand on the button in a 3-handed game. It is well ahead of the range of hands the blinds should be defending with. A 3x raise to 300 chips allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately, or play a pot in position if called.

I have AQs in the small blind facing a button open-raise from Flash. This is a premium starting hand that dominates a wide button opening range. 3-betting from the small blind is optimal to extract value, thin the field (forcing the Big Blind out), and take control of the hand. I'll size my 3-bet to 1100 to give the out-of-position Flash a tough decision with marginal hands.

J3 offsuit is an extremely weak hand with very little playability. Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind after an opening raise from the Button, there is no profitable way to continue. Calling would be committing chips out of position against a strong range, and bluffing (4-betting) with such a poor holding is high-risk and unnecessary at this stage of the tournament. The most disciplined and +EV decision is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I opened K8o from the button and faced a significant 3-bet from the Small Blind. While I would have position post-flop, K8o is near the bottom of my opening range and is easily dominated by Pro's 3-betting range (hands like AK, KQ, KJ, and pocket pairs). Calling 800 more into a 1500 pot requires roughly 35% equity, which K8o likely lacks against a standard 3-bet range. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Seven-three offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand with virtually no playability or equity. Even though I have position as the Button, playing this hand is highly unprofitable. Folding is the only sensible choice.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (2-3 offsuit). Playing this hand offers poor equity and will force me to play out of position against the big blind for the rest of the hand. It is standard to fold such weak, unsuited, and disconnected cards to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Js 2h is a weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position relative to the blinds later in the hand or just generally unsuited for a raise-to-open from the button. Folding is the most disciplined and effective strategy to preserve my stack for a stronger starting hand.

Qd 3s is a weak hand to play from the Small Blind, especially out of position against the Big Blind. While blind-vs-blind situations allow for wider ranges, this hand doesn't have enough equity or playability to justify a call or a raise. I'll fold and look for a more advantageous hand.

I am on the button with Qh 5s. While a three-handed game allows for wider opening ranges from the button, Q5o is generally considered too weak to open, even in this position. The risk of being re-raised or called and forced to play a weak hand out of position (relative to the blind that calls) outweighs the potential 150 chip gain from stealing the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with J7o. Flash folded on the Button. Although it's a blind vs blind situation, J7 offsuit is quite weak to play out of position against the Big Blind. The safest and most standard action here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

K6o is a weak offsuit hand, even on the button in a 3-handed game. It lacks playability post-flop and can easily be dominated by better Kings or medium pairs. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

With QJs in the Small Blind and the Button having folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. QJs is a high-equity hand, and raising is the best approach to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. Raising to 300 is a standard size that allows me to build the pot and control the narrative.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8d 6d, a solid suited connector. Flashlight (Small Blind) raised to 300 after the Button folded. I have to call 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 2:1 pot odds. More importantly, since the Button folded, I will have direct position on the Small Blind for the remainder of the hand. 86s plays well in position and has sufficient equity against a typical SB steal range. Calling is the most standard and profitable play here to realize my equity and leverage my positional advantage post-flop.

The flop of 4h 5c 3c is extremely well-connected and highly favorable to the Big Blind's range, which likely includes many low-to-mid cards, small pairs, and straight draws. My Queen-Jack high provides no pair or meaningful draw, and betting into this board out of position is disadvantageous. Checking is the most strategic play to control the pot size and proceed cautiously, as I am currently losing to almost any pair or better that the opponent may have.

I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) on a low, connected board that likely favors my calling range more than the Small Blind's raising range. Since Flashlight checked, I will bet half-pot (300) to take initiative, capitalize on fold equity against overcards, and build a pot in case I hit my draw on the turn.

The flop of 4h 5c 3c is extremely well-connected and hits the Big Blind's calling range significantly harder than my own. With only Queen-Jack high, I have effectively zero equity against any made pair or draw, and being out of position makes it difficult to maneuver on later streets. Given the board texture and my lack of a piece of this board, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for more favorable situations.

I am on the button with 6c7c, which are suited connectors with decent playability. In a three-handed game, this is a strong hand to open the pot with to apply pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. A raise to 250 is a standard size to steal the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Hearts and Three of Diamonds (T3o). The button has raised to 250. T3o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Even though I only need to call 200 more, the hand is well below the threshold for defending the blind in this spot. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Holding 6s9h offsuit in the Big Blind facing a 2.5x raise from the Button. This hand is far too weak to defend out of position, even with decent pot odds, due to low equity and terrible reverse implied odds.

I am on the button with 9-6 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand. While I am in position, 96o does not play well post-flop and doesn't have much equity against a typical calling range from the blinds. Standard play in a 3-handed tournament is to fold trash hands from the button and wait for better spots to utilize position.

Q8s is a solid suited gapper and plays well post-flop. Since the Button folded, I am in a Blind vs Blind situation. Raising to 3x (300) is a standard steal attempt that puts pressure on the Big Blind, while also building a pot with a hand that has good playability if called.

I hold pocket threes in the big blind against a small blind open. Pocket pairs have great implied odds for set-mining, and calling is a standard approach here to see a flop given the pot odds and stack depth. I will call the 200 to complete my total investment of 300 in the pot for this street.

We completely missed the flop and have almost no equity or backdoor draws with Qh8h on a K-T-2 two-diamond board. The board connects well with the Big Blind's calling range, which likely includes broadway cards, Kx, Tx, and flush draws. Making a continuation bet out of position with air here is unprofitable. Checking with the intention to evaluate (and likely fold to a bet) is the standard and safest play.

I am in the big blind with a pair of threes on a K-T-2 board. My opponent (Pro) checked to me after raising pre-flop. While it is possible they are checking to trap, it is more likely they have missed or are playing cautiously with air. Betting here doesn't accomplish much, as I will likely be called by any better hand and fold out hands I am already beating. Checking is the best way to control the pot size and realize equity to potentially improve or take the pot down later if the turn checks through.

The turn card brings the Ace of Diamonds, completing potential flush draws and placing four broadway cards on the board. While we picked up a gutshot straight draw (any Jack gives us a straight), the board is extremely dangerous, and we hold no diamonds. Since we have almost no showdown value and a bluff is unlikely to succeed against a range that often contains an Ace, King, Ten, or a diamond, checking and being ready to fold to any bet is the best option.

The board texture is highly coordinated with three diamonds (2d, Td, Ad) and high cards (A, K). My pocket threes are effectively a weak bluff-catcher with very low equity. Since the opponent checked the turn, checking behind allows me to see the river for free, minimizing risk while avoiding a bluff into a range that could easily contain an Ace, a King, or a flush. It is the most prudent path to control the pot size.

On the river, the board remains very wet and connected, and we have absolutely no showdown value with Queen high. Even though our opponent checked the flop and turn, their checking range still includes many hands that beat us, such as a Ten, a weak King, or low pocket pairs. Attempting a bluff into a board featuring an Ace, King, Ten, and three diamonds is risky and could easily be picked off by a stubborn pair. The most prudent play is to check, concede the pot, and fold to any bet.

I am at showdown with pocket threes on a board of A-K-T-7-2. There is an Ace and a King on the board, making my pocket pair extremely weak. I have no incentive to bet as I will not get called by worse, and my opponent's check could be a trap or simply a surrender with air. Checking behind allows me to go to showdown and potentially win against a total air bluff, while minimizing any further loss.

With pocket fours on the button, this is a standard open-raise. We have a low pocket pair which benefits from positional advantage and has great set-mining value if called. Raising to 2.5x the big blind applies pressure to the blinds to steal the pot while keeping the investment reasonable if we face a 3-bet.

I am in the small blind with Jc 6c. Pro opened on the button, and the pot odds are favorable to call. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, suited connectors have good playability and allow me to see a flop. Folding would be too passive given the stack depth, and a 3-bet is unnecessary risk against a standard button open.

I'm in the Big Blind with King-Six offsuit. The Button (Pro) raised to 250, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) called. I need to call 150 more to see the flop, which gives me 4:1 pot odds (150 to win 600). King-high has reasonable equity against the wide ranges of a Button raiser and a flat-calling Small Blind. Since I am closing the pre-flop action and getting a great price, calling is the most standard play here.

The board comes down A-K-2, which is extremely favorable for the pre-flop raiser (Pro) who is in position. My hand (Jc 6c) has no pair and only a backdoor flush draw, offering very little equity. Betting into two opponents on this texture would be a significant overplay. The standard, professional move here is to check and evaluate the action, intending to fold if there is significant aggression.

I have middle pair (Kings) with a weak kicker on an Ace-high board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) and another player (Flashlight), checking is the most prudent move. I want to see how Pro reacts to this flop. If Pro bets, I'll have to decide if my King is good enough to call based on the bet sizing, but for now, checking is the standard play for a middle pair on this texture.

The flop of Ac Ks 2c is very hostile to pocket fours, particularly in a multi-way pot. The opponents' pre-flop calling ranges are heavily saturated with Ax, Kx, broadway cards, and club flush draws. A continuation bet here would have very little fold equity and would just be burning chips against hands that are virtually guaranteed to call or raise. Checking back allows us to see a free turn card, hoping to hit a miraculous 2-outer or gracefully give up if faced with aggression on later streets.

The board is paired with the deuce of spades on the turn, and I have Jack-high with a backdoor flush draw. Given that the flop was checked around by all three players, there is no reason to lead out here. I am out of position and have minimal showdown value. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free river card, or fold if there is significant aggression from the other players.

The turn brings a second deuce, pairing the board. My hand remains middle pair (Kings) with a weak kicker. Given that all players checked the flop, it's possible that no one holds an Ace, though someone could be slow-playing. By checking, I continue to control the size of the pot and see how the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) acts. I have decent showdown value, but my hand is not strong enough to bet for value into two opponents on an Ace-high board.

The turn card pairs the deuce, making the board Ac Ks 2c 2s. Both opponents have checked again, which often indicates weakness, draws, or marginal hands trying to get to showdown cheaply. While we could try to bluff here representing a slow-played Ace or King, our pocket fours still have a tiny bit of showdown value against missed draws. More importantly, bluffing into two opponents on an Ace-King board is risky, as one of them could easily be trapping or pot-controlling with a hand like weak Ax or Kx that will never fold. The safest and most prudent play is to check behind and take a free river card.

The board is A-K-2-2-9, and my Jack-high holds no showdown value. Both opponents checked the flop and the turn, which suggests that no one has connected strongly with this board, but any pair or better will beat my hand. Betting would be a pure bluff into two opponents, which is ill-advised given that neither player has shown any aggression. Checking is the most prudent action to minimize potential losses, as I cannot win the pot at showdown with this hand unless both opponents have absolute air, which is unlikely given their pre-flop call.

The river is a 9s, making the final board 2c-Ks-Ac-2s-9s. All players have checked both the flop and the turn. I have second pair (Kings) with a 6 kicker. It is highly unlikely anyone has an Ace or a 2 given the passive action. Flashlight has checked to me on the river. While my King is likely the best hand, betting might only get called by better (Ace) or a better King, and fold out weaker hands that I already beat. However, checking back allows me to realize my showdown value for free. Given the pot size and the action so far, a check is the safest and most standard way to proceed to showdown.

Both opponents have checked to us on the river. The board is Ac Ks 2c 2s 9s, and we hold pocket fours. We beat missed club and straight draws, but lose to almost any pair that might have been checking down. Bluffing into two opponents on this board is highly risky and unnecessary, as one of them could easily look us up with a weak pair, like a 9 or a stubbornly played King. Checking behind allows us to see a free showdown and occasionally win against busted draws.

Queen-Deuce offsuit is a very weak starting hand, especially in a 3-handed game. Even on the button, there is no strategic value in opening with such a marginal hand. The best course of action is to fold and maintain discipline, waiting for a stronger hand to enter the pot.

In this small blind versus big blind situation, I am holding Ten-Trey suited. While it's tempting to complete the bet or raise to steal the pot, T3s is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. 'Pro' in the big blind has shown some aggression in previous hands. Folding here is a disciplined play to preserve my chip lead and avoid being put in a difficult spot post-flop with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with A2o in a 3-handed game. An Ace-high hand is well within the standard opening range from this position. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take control of the pot and utilize my positional advantage against the blinds.

Facing a 3x raise from the Button, 4d 3d is too weak of a suited connector to call from the Small Blind out of position. While it has some playability, it will rarely flop strong enough to justify continuing, and being out of position makes post-flop play difficult. A 3-bet bluff is also suboptimal compared to hands with better blockers (like Axs) or higher equity suited connectors. Folding is the safest and most profitable long-term decision.

I have been dealt pocket Kings in the big blind, which is an extremely strong hand. Flash has opened from the button, which is a wide range. I want to build the pot and take the initiative, so I will 3-bet to 1000 to isolate the button and maximize value.

I opened the button with A2o and was faced with a 3-bet from the big blind after the small blind folded. While A2o is a decent opening hand shorthanded, it plays poorly against a 3-bet range from the big blind, especially when the raise is quite substantial (from 300 to 1000). Flashlight has shown aggression in previous hands and a 3-bet here typically represents a strong range of pairs and high Broadway cards that dominate A2o. Calling out of position (though I have absolute position post-flop, the SPR will be low) with a weak Ace is often a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

I have Pocket Tens on the button in a 3-handed game, which is a premium hand. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot, take control, and extract value from worse hands while protecting my equity.

I am holding 5-2 offsuit, which is among the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Being in the Small Blind against a raise from the button puts me out of position for the rest of the hand. There is no mathematical or strategic justification to call or raise with this hand. Therefore, folding is the clear and correct decision.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. In a 3-handed game, the Button's opening range is typically very wide, and J8o is strong enough to defend given the pot odds. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, requiring roughly 31% equity to break even. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding J8o to a standard button open in this format would be too tight. I will call to see a flop.

I have middle pair with a Jack kicker on a relatively dry board of K-8-6. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (the Button), the most standard and effective play is to check to them. This allows them to continuation bet with their bluffs or weaker hands while I control the size of the pot with my showdown value hand. Folding would be too weak, and leading (donk betting) here doesn't achieve much on a board that generally favors the Button's range. I will check and likely call a standard-sized bet.

The board is K-8-6 rainbow, which is a very dry texture. I have second pair with Pocket Tens. Betting a small size (around 1/3 pot) is optimal here to deny equity to overcards like AQ, AJ, or QJ, and to extract value from smaller pairs, underpairs, or bottom pair. This c-bet also helps define my opponent's range while keeping the pot manageable. If I get check-raised, I can comfortably let it go.

I have middle pair (Eights) with a Jack kicker on a relatively dry board. The Button (Pro) has placed a small continuation bet of 225 into a pot of 650 (about 35% pot). My hand is too strong to fold given the pot odds (requiring only about 20% equity) and the wide range the Button is expected to be betting here. I will call to keep the pot size controlled and see how the turn card changes the board texture, as raising might force out hands I'm beating and isolate me against Kings or better.

On the turn, I have improved to trips (three of a kind) with Eights. The board is now King-Eight-Six-Eight. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop and flop aggressor (Pro), the best strategic play is to check once more. This allows my opponent to continue with their aggression if they are bluffing or value-betting a King. Leading out might scare away weaker hands that I currently beat, whereas checking maintains the deception of my hand strength and allows me to see how Pro responds to the board pairing.

The turn pairs the 8, which means the board is now K-8-6-8. Checking behind is the best option here for pot control. Betting will mostly get called by better hands like Kx and 8x, which are both firmly in the big blind's flop check-calling range. By checking, I keep the pot small with a marginal showdown value hand (underpair to the King), avoid getting check-raised by an 8, and potentially induce a bluff from missed straight draws or floats on the river.

I have trips (Eights). The Button (Pro) checked back on the turn after betting the flop, which strongly suggests they do not have an Eight and are likely holding a King or a middle pocket pair. By leading the river, I can extract value from these hands. A bet of around 60% of the pot is large enough to get paid but small enough to look like a possible missed draw or a thin value bet, encouraging a call from a King.

Flash leads out for 650 on the river after defending the big blind, check-calling a small flop c-bet, and seeing the turn check through. This line is very consistent with a King that wants to extract value from a middle pair or an 8 that improved on the turn. While there are some missed straight draws like 97 or 75 in Flash's range, typical opponents under-bluff in this spot. Flash's range is heavily weighted towards value hands (Kx and 8x) that float the flop and value bet the river. Since my pocket Tens do not beat any of his value range and I have better hands in my own range to call with (such as Kx that checked the turn), folding is the most disciplined and optimal play.

7-3 offsuit is among the weakest possible starting hands in No-Limit Texas Hold'em. While being on the button grants positional advantage, the hand's raw equity is insufficient to justify investing chips, especially with two active opponents who may defend their blinds. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for more profitable situations.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s 5h. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). 65o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a competent opponent. Given the stack sizes and the tournament stage, I prefer to fold this hand rather than complete the bet or raise, as I will be out of position for the rest of the hand with poor equity.

I am on the button with 7-4 suited. While 3-handed play warrants a wider opening range from the button, 7-4 suited is near the bottom of that range. My opponents, particularly Flashlight in the big blind, have shown a willingness to be active and 3-bet. Given my current chip lead and the relatively low value of this hand, I will fold to avoid unnecessary complications and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 2s, which is 7-2 offsuit, traditionally the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With the Button folding, I am first to act against the Big Blind. There is no reason to put more chips into the pot with this hand. It is an easy fold.

J9 offsuit is a weak hand even from the button in a 3-handed game. Opening here would often be a marginal steal attempt that plays poorly post-flop against a caller or 3-bettor. It's better to fold and conserve chips for better starting hands.

Ace-Jack offsuit is a strong holding for a heads-up situation in the small blind, especially after the button has folded. Raising to 300 puts effective pressure on the big blind, allowing me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or gain control of the hand with a stronger range.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit (T3o). The Small Blind has raised to 300. While I am getting 2:1 odds to call, T3o is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. I have a healthy stack and there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand against a standard 3x open. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with suited connectors (Jack-Ten of hearts). This is a strong, playable hand in late position. Raising to 300 is a standard button open size to capitalize on position, exert pressure on the blinds, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or set up a favorable post-flop scenario.

I have 72 offsuit, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Flashlight opened from the button for 3x, and calling out of position with such a weak hand is a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with J4o, a very weak and unconnected offsuit hand. The Button (Flashlight) raised to 3 big blinds. This hand has very poor playability post-flop and little equity against a standard Button opening range, making a fold the only profitable decision here.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit (JTo) in a 3-handed game. This is a strong hand to open with from this position to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot immediately or play a post-flop game with positional advantage. A standard 3x big blind raise is appropriate here.

My hand is Queen-Four offsuit (Q4o), which is extremely weak and highly disconnected. Facing a 3x raise from the button while out of position in the small blind, folding is the only profitable play. It's a standard pre-flop fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 5h, which is a very weak hand with poor equity against a Button open. Calling 200 to play a hand out of position with weak cards is not mathematically sound. The best strategy is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

A9 suited is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game, especially when acting first from the Button. It is definitely worth an open-raise to take the betting initiative and potentially steal the blinds, while also having good playability post-flop if called.

I hold Ace-King offsuit, a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Pro opened on the button, and I am in the small blind. I want to take initiative, bloat the pot, and narrow the field against the button. A 3-bet is the standard and most profitable play here to gain control, rather than just calling and playing out of position post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding J5o. The Button (Pro) opened for 250 and the Small Blind (Flashlight) 3-bet to 850. J5o is a very weak hand that is significantly behind both an opening range and a 3-betting range. There is no reason to commit any chips to this pot. I will fold.

We opened with A9s from the Button and are facing a very large 3-bet (3.4x) from the Small Blind. While A9s is a strong starting hand with good playability in position, it frequently finds itself dominated by the value portion of a 3-betting range, such as ATs+, AJo+, and medium to large pocket pairs. Calling here requires a significant portion of our stack with a marginal holding, so folding and preserving our chips for a better spot is the safest and most disciplined approach.

K6o is a weak, easily dominated hand in a three-handed game. Despite being on the button, I want to avoid committing chips to a marginal hand that will likely have to play in a multi-way pot or face aggression from the blinds. Folding allows me to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 3d, which is a very weak starting hand. With Flashlight folding on the button, it is just me and Pro (Big Blind). Although it only costs 50 more to call, 8-3 offsuit has very little post-flop equity and playing it out of position is a losing strategy long-term. Folding is the standard professional play here to conserve chips.

I am on the button with King-Four offsuit. While King-high hands are often open-raising candidates in a three-handed game to steal the blinds, K4o is at the very bottom of that range. Given that both the Small Blind (Pro) and Big Blind (Flashlight) have shown a willingness to 3-bet or play aggressively against my opens in previous hands (Hand 6, Hand 15, Hand 22), I will choose a more conservative approach and fold this weak hand to avoid being put in a difficult spot out of position or facing a 3-bet.

With Ace-Jack offsuit in the small blind and the button folding, I have a very strong hand for a blind-versus-blind confrontation. Raising to 3x the big blind is a standard play to extract value, take the betting lead, and put pressure on the big blind.

I am in the big blind with A6s, which is a strong enough hand to defend against a small blind open. The pot odds (paying 200 to win a total pot of 400) are favorable for a call. Given the stack depths and the fact that I am in position relative to the SB, calling is the most balanced and efficient play to realize my hand's equity.

I missed the flop entirely, holding only Ace-high with a very weak backdoor flush draw. While the Queen-high board generally connects well with my pre-flop raising range from the small blind, the board is also quite coordinated with heart and straight draws that the big blind could easily have. Out of position, checking is a solid play to control the pot size and look for a free turn card. If my opponent bets, I can easily fold without committing more chips.

The board is dry and rainbow, and I have Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. Pro checked to me, which suggests they might be looking for a free card or are playing cautiously. Betting now likely folds out hands that I'm already ahead of while getting called by better hands. Checking allows me to realize my equity for free and keeps the pot size under control.

This suggests a very weak range or a missed overcard hand. Since I have improved to a straight, I must bet for value. A bet of roughly 2/3 the pot (400 into 600) is a standard size that can get called by any pair (like a 9, 8, or even a small pocket pair) or an Ace-high that suspects a missed draw/bluff, while maximizing my return on a board where my hand is very likely the best.

The opponent (Flash) has raised on the river after checking the flop and turn. My hand is King-high with no pair. While I have been checking throughout the hand, this river bet by Flash is likely a polarized bluff or a value bet with a small pair that decided to start betting. Given the board texture (3-9-2-8-4), almost any pair beats my King-high. Calling here with just King-high is not profitable as my opponent's range has enough made pairs that I cannot beat. I must fold.

I am on the button in a three-handed game. With K-3 offsuit, while not a premium hand, it is strong enough to attempt a blind steal from the button position. A standard raise to 300 chips is a typical open to win the 150 pot pre-flop or take control of the hand.

Ace-Nine offsuit is a strong hand in three-handed play, particularly against a standard button opening. By three-betting, I aim to take down the pot immediately or thin the field and gain the initiative heading into the flop. Calling from the small blind often leads to a multi-way pot out of position, so a raise is the more aggressive and profitable play here. I will raise to 1,000 to put pressure on both the button and the big blind.
Facing an open from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, 2s 4d is an extremely weak hand and highly unprofitable to play, especially out of position. Folding is the only correct decision.

K-3 suited is a marginal hand that I opened from the button for a blind steal. After being 3-bet to 1000, calling with such a weak hand is generally unprofitable as my opponent is representing significant strength. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the button with Ace-King offsuit, one of the strongest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. My stack is healthy, and I'm currently the chip leader. Standard strategy dictates an opening raise from the button to put pressure on the blinds and build value. I'll raise to 3x the big blind, which has been the standard opening size throughout the tournament.

Facing a button raise with QJo in the Small Blind, we are out of position with a marginal holding. While Flash opens frequently and their range might be wide, playing QJo from the blinds out of position often leads to tricky post-flop situations and reverse implied odds. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play to preserve chips for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (3-6 offsuit). Despite the odds being decent to call (200 to win 450), there is no value in playing this hand against an aggressive opponent in position. I will fold and save my chips for a better opportunity.

I am dealt 62 offsuit on the button, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given the poor hand strength and lack of potential, the standard and correct play is to fold pre-flop.

Holding 9-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand. As the Small Blind, I am out of position against the Big Blind. Folding is the most disciplined play to conserve my stack rather than investing more chips into a marginal situation with a poor hand.

K-Q offsuit is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game, particularly on the button. Raising here allows me to define my range, potentially steal the blinds, or take control of the hand if called. 300 is a standard sizing to open, keeping the pot manageable while putting pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for a standard 3x raise. While I have the chip lead, Ten-Eight offsuit is a weak hand to defend out of position, especially with a competent player in the Big Blind yet to act. Calling often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I'll be out of position with a marginal hand. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better spots.

A2s is a strong hand that plays well post-flop, especially heads-up. Facing a standard button open from Flashlight, calling from the big blind is a profitable play, as A2s can make the nut flush, wheel straights, and has reasonable top-pair potential. A 3-bet is also an option, but calling keeps the variance lower and allows me to see a flop with a highly playable hand.

The flop is Kh-9c-3s, which is a fairly dry board that heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. Flashlight can easily have a lot of King-x hands, sets, or strong broadways. With Ah-2h, I have an overcard and a backdoor nut flush draw, but no immediate made hand or strong draw to justify leading out (donk betting). The standard and most strategic play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their action.

I have top pair with a good kicker on a dry, rainbow board. Pro is in the big blind and checked to me. This is a classic continuation bet spot to extract value from any middle pairs or lower kings in Pro's range. The board is not particularly coordinated, making it unlikely Pro has hit a strong draw, so a standard sizing of 300 into the 650 pot should induce calls from hands I dominate.

Facing a continuation bet on a dry Kh-9c-3s board, our Ah-2h only has an overcard and a backdoor nut flush draw. This is not enough equity to continue profitably against a standard c-bet from the pre-flop aggressor, whose range connects well with the King. Calling would be too loose, and raising as a bluff is unnecessary given our weak holding and positional disadvantage. Folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving play.

I am on the button with Ten of Spades and Five of Clubs (T5o). While 3-handed play allows for a wider opening range, T5o is exceptionally weak and sits near the bottom of my range. Pro (SB) has shown willingness to 3-bet in previous hands, and Flashlight (BB) is fairly active. With a comfortable chip lead and 117 big blinds, there is no need to take an unnecessary risk with a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Queen-four offsuit is a very weak hand. Being in the small blind, playing out of position against the big blind with such weak holdings is unprofitable in the long run. I will simply fold and conserve my stack.

K2o is a very weak hand to play, even from the button in a 3-handed game. It has terrible equity when called and lacks playability post-flop. The standard and optimal play is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

Holding Ten-Trey offsuit in the small blind is a very weak starting hand. With a weak hand out of position against an aggressive Big Blind opponent, it is disciplined to fold rather than invest chips to see a flop that will likely miss or be difficult to navigate.

I am holding 5s 9h on the button. This is a very weak, unsuited hand that does not play well in a multi-way pot or against aggression. Given the pot size and the strength of my hand, there is no strategic value in calling the 100 chips to play. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with J4o. Flashlight has already folded from the Button. The pot is small (150) and it's only 50 chips more for me to call into a pot that would then be 200, but J4o is a very weak hand to play out of position against Pro, who has been reasonably active. While the price to complete is attractive, J4o doesn't flop well and I'd be playing from the worst position for the rest of the hand. I'll simply fold and wait for a better spot, especially since I have a healthy stack of 11,725.

I am on the button with 9s6c. This is a weak starting hand. While I could attempt to steal the blinds, both Pro and Flashlight have shown some willingness to defend or 3-bet. With a stack of 11,725 and being mid-way through the tournament (Hand 90/150), there is no need to play trash hands out of position or in marginal spots. Folding here is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

7h 3c offsuit is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Being in the small blind, out of position post-flop, there's no reason to commit any chips with this holding. An easy fold.

K5 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability post-flop. Although I am on the button, this hand does not have enough equity to justify an open raise. The best and most standard play is to fold and conserve my stack for better spots.

With pocket sixes in the small blind against the big blind after the button folds, I have a strong equity advantage. Raising is the standard move to take initiative, pressure the opponent, and potentially win the pot immediately or see a flop with the betting lead. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to define the range and protect my hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Hearts and Six of Hearts. The Button (Pro) has folded, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 2:1 odds. Suited connectors/one-gappers like T6s are strong enough to defend the Big Blind in a heads-up situation against the Small Blind, especially since I will have position throughout the rest of the hand. Calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my positional advantage without bloating the pot unnecessarily pre-flop.

The board texture is dry, featuring a queen and two lower cards. While my pocket sixes are an underpair, I am the pre-flop aggressor and should continue with a continuation bet to maintain initiative. A bet of 300 represents roughly half the pot and is likely to make my opponent fold hands like low suited connectors or high-card junk that missed entirely. If I am called, I can reassess the turn.

On a flop of 5h-Qh-3c, I have a flush draw with my Ten and Six of hearts. Flashlight (Small Blind) has led out with a bet of 300 into a 600-chip pot. I am getting 3:1 pot odds on a call (or 4:1 immediate odds relative to the final pot size), and since I have a roughly 19% chance to hit my flush on the turn, calling is mathematically justified, especially with the added benefit of being in position for the rest of the hand. I will call to see the turn and evaluate based on whether the flush completes or if the board texture changes significantly.

On the turn, the board has filled out to show a 5-Q-3-4 texture. My pocket sixes have picked up an open-ended straight draw (need a 2 or 7). My pair of sixes is relatively weak against a calling range, as it is vulnerable to any pair of sevens or better, but still has some showdown value. Betting again into a board that just connected well with many of the opponent's calling hands is risky, especially since the turn card helps the opponent's calling range (lots of 2s, 6s, 7s, or 4s). Checking allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity, keeping the pot manageable while potentially seeing a free river card. If my opponent bets, I can re-evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the most balanced approach.

On the turn, the board is 5h-Qh-3c-4c. I am holding the Ten and Six of hearts, giving me a flush draw (9 outs) and an open-ended straight draw (8 outs for 2-3-4-5-6 or 3-4-5-6-7). With a total of 15 unique outs, I have approximately 32% equity to improve to a very strong hand on the river. Flashlight (the Small Blind) has checked, which may indicate a lack of strength or an attempt to pot-control with a marginal hand like a Queen or a middle pair. Since I am in position, checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and see the river without the risk of being check-raised off my draw. If I hit my hand on the river, I can then bet for value.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket sixes on a 5-Q-3-4-3 board. My hand is a pair of sixes, which has showdown value but is likely behind any Queen, any five, or a three. The turn and river were checked through by both players, signaling a lack of aggression. Betting here is turning my hand into a bluff, which is likely unnecessary as I have some showdown value against missed draws or lower-card junk. Checking is the most prudent play to reach showdown and minimize losses if I am beaten or potentially win if the opponent also has a lower pair or just high-card junk.

On the river, the board has completed my flush with the Trey of Hearts. The final board is 5h-Qh-3c-4c-3h, and I am holding Th-6h, giving me a Ten-high flush (Qh-Th-6h-5h-3h). Although the board paired on the river, creating a possibility for a full house, Flashlight's check on both the turn and the river strongly suggests a marginal hand like a Queen or a middle pair that is looking for a cheap showdown, rather than a monster hand. I have a very strong hand that should be bet for value. A bet of 750 (approximately 62.5% of the pot) is large enough to extract value from a Queen or an Ace-high hand while remaining small enough to potentially get called by a wider range of the opponent's holdings.

The opponent checked the turn and the river, then bet 750 into a 1200 pot. This line is often indicative of a polarized range—either a trapped strong hand (like a Queen or a 5) or a complete bluff with a missed draw or a weak hand that has no showdown value. With pocket sixes, I have two pair (sixes and threes) with a Queen kicker. I beat 4-4 and 2-2, and I have good equity against any bluffing range. Given the pot odds (requiring ~28% equity), calling is a mathematically sound decision to protect against the high frequency of missed draws in the opponent's range, such as 2-x or other unpaired hands that are turning into bluffs.

My holding of Ten-Five offsuit is extremely weak and lacks playability, especially in a 3-handed game. There is no strategic justification for investing chips into the pot with such a low-equity hand from the button.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 4c, which is a very weak starting hand. The Button (Flashlight) has folded. I am on the button with suited one-gappers (8d6d), which is a strong hand to open in a three-handed game. Raising to 300 (3x) is consistent with my previous betting patterns and allows me to take the initiative with position. This puts pressure on the blinds and gives me the chance to win the pot immediately or see a flop with a hand that has good playability.

My hand is Queen-Two offsuit, which is an extremely weak and disconnected starting hand. I am facing a 3x raise from the Button while sitting in the Small Blind, meaning I will be out of position post-flop if I call. Defending such a poor hand in this spot is highly unprofitable, so the correct and standard decision is to fold.

I am in the big blind with 5-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite the decent pot odds offered (needing to call 200 into a 650 pot), this hand has very poor playability and equity against a button opening range. It is mathematically and strategically correct to fold here rather than chase with a dominated hand, conserving my stack for better opportunities.

Qs Ts is a strong suited connector/gapper that plays very well in position. Opening with a raise from the button is a standard play to steal the blinds or take the initiative into the post-flop. A 2.5x raise size is standard here.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (5h 4d) and the button has opened. Calling out of position with this hand is negative expected value, especially with the big blind still to act. It is better to conserve chips for a more favorable spot.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7d 8s. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 150 more to see a flop. The pot odds are approximately 3.6:1 (150 to win 400). 78-offsuit is a reasonable hand to defend the Big Blind with against a standard Button opening range, especially given the stack depths and the price. I will call to see the flop and play from there.

On this Ace-King high flop with a spade draw, I have completely missed the board. As the big blind who called a pre-flop raise, my range is generally weaker than the button's opening range on such a coordinated high-card board. I will check to the aggressor and likely fold to any significant bet, as I have no real equity or draws to continue with.

With Qs Ts on a Kc As 9s board, I have a massive combo draw (flush draw + gutshot straight draw). This board structure strongly favors my pre-flop raising range, as I can easily represent an Ace or a King. A continuation bet of around half to two-thirds pot will apply pressure on marginal hands, build the pot for when my draw hits, and maintain the betting initiative.

On a board of Kc As 9s, I have absolutely no equity with 7-high and no draws. The board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which includes many Ax, Kx, and spade-flush draws. Calling here would be a waste of chips given the lack of any potential improvement on future streets.

T7o is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability and showdown value. Even on the button in a three-handed game, this hand is frequently dominated by the blinds' wider calling ranges. Preserving my stack of 8525 is a priority, and folding is the most disciplined decision given the marginal strength of these cards.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Flashlight has folded on the button. Pro is a competent opponent in the Big Blind, but I should definitely be raising for value and to take control of the hand. A standard 3x raise to 300 has been consistent in this game and is the optimal choice to build the pot while potentially taking it down pre-flop.

Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, A5o is a marginal hand. Even though I would have positional advantage post-flop, an offsuit weak Ace has reverse implied odds against a raising range. It's safer to fold and look for a better spot to put chips in the pot, preserving my 84.5 BB stack.

I am on the button with Kc 8c, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Opening with a raise is standard to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early. Based on the previous hands, a 3x big blind raise (300 chips) has been effective for me.

I am in the Small Blind facing a Button raise. My hand is K5 offsuit, which is quite weak and plays poorly out of position. The standard and most profitable play here is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

T9 offsuit is a marginal hand that struggles to perform well when played out of position against an aggressive opponent. Given the depth of my stack and the stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold and preserve my chips for a more favorable situation rather than defending with a weak range in the big blind.

I am dealt 4-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Even though I have the positional advantage on the button, this hand has virtually no playability or equity to justify opening the pot. The mathematically sound and standard decision is to fold.

I am in the small blind with a strong starting hand (Ace-Eight offsuit) in a blind-versus-blind situation. Since the button has folded, I have a great opportunity to take the initiative by raising. This pressures the big blind and allows me to potentially take down the pot immediately or play with the betting lead in position post-flop.

Flashlight has been consistently raising from the Small Blind when the action folds to them. Queen-Seven offsuit is a reasonable defending hand in a 3-handed game, particularly when receiving 2:1 pot odds and closing the pre-flop action with the advantage of position for the rest of the hand.

As the pre-flop aggressor, maintaining the initiative with a continuation bet is the standard approach to apply pressure and either win the pot immediately or build equity for when I hit my draw.

I have middle pair (Sevens) with a Queen kicker on a 7-9-4 board. Flashlight's 350 bet into a 600 pot is a standard continuation bet from the Small Blind. Given the wide ranges in a heads-up blind-versus-blind battle, middle pair is too strong to fold. I will call to see the turn, keeping the pot manageable while allowing the opponent to continue bluffing with weaker hands.

The board has paired on the turn with the 4h, which may have improved the opponent's hand if they held a 4 or an overpair. My Ace-high is currently weak and I lack a strong draw, so firing another barrel is high-risk. Checking allows me to re-evaluate based on the opponent's action, keeping the pot size manageable while I hold a marginal hand out of position.

Flashlight's check on the turn after a flop continuation bet often indicates a shift to pot control or a missed draw. My middle pair (Sevens) with a Queen kicker is a strong showdown-value hand but remains vulnerable to any 9 or a 4. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and can comfortably evaluate the river. Betting risks a check-raise from a stronger hand like a 9 or a 4, while checking back allows me to reach showdown or induce a bluff on the river.

I have rivered the nut flush (Ace-high) on a board of 7c-9c-4d-4h-6c. My hand is currently the effective nuts, as the only hands beating me are full houses (specifically 4-4 or combinations of 4 with pocket pairs), which are relatively unlikely given the action. I need to extract value from any lower flushes, straights, or two-pair combinations Flash might hold. A bet of 750 into a 1300 pot represents a strong value bet that is likely to be called by second-best hands.

On the river, the board is 7c-9c-4d-4h-6c. I have two pair, Sevens and Fours, with a Queen kicker. Flashlight's line (bet flop, check turn, bet river) is polarized. While the 6c completes several draws, including flushes and an 8-5 straight, I hold the Queen of Clubs (Qc), which serves as an important blocker to the flushes Flashlight might be representing. Given that this was a blind-versus-blind battle, ranges are extremely wide. The pot odds are approximately 2.7 to 1 (750 to win a total pot of 2800), meaning I only need to be correct about 21% of the time to break even. Given the wide range of possible bluffs from a Small Blind opener on a scary river card and my blocker, calling to catch a bluff is the optimal play.

Ten-Jack suited is a strong, playable hand in the button position. Raising allows me to take the betting initiative, isolate the blinds, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or build a pot in position if called. A standard open raise size of 300 is appropriate here.
