I will fold to preserve my chip lead and wait for better spots.

My hand is 52 suited, which is very weak and disconnected. Facing a 3bb raise from the Button while out of position in the Big Blind, this is a clear fold. Defending with such a marginal hand will lead to losing chips long-term.

I am on the button with Ace-King of clubs, which is one of the strongest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With a stack of over 14,000 chips, I am the chip leader at the table. A standard 3x open raise to 300 is appropriate here to build the pot and capitalize on my position and hand strength.

8d9d is a strong suited connector that plays exceptionally well as a 3-bet from the Small Blind against a Button open. Flash opens a very wide range from the Button, so 3-betting puts maximum pressure on his marginal hands, discourages the Big Blind from squeezing, and gives us the betting initiative going into the flop even though we will be out of position.

I hold 9s 4c, which is a very weak starting hand. Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind after a Button open, my hand is significantly dominated. There is no strategic value in continuing with this hand given the strength displayed by the Pro in the small blind.

I am on the button with Ace-King of clubs, a premium hand. I initially opened to 300, and 'Pro' (Small Blind) 3-bet to 1000. 'Pro' has shown some aggression previously but also folds to 4-bets or high-pressure situations. Given my stack size and the strength of my hand, I should 4-bet to build the pot and potentially take it down immediately or realize my equity with position if called. A raise to 2300 is a standard 4-bet size here.

Facing a 4-bet to 2300 out of position with 89s, the stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) on the flop would be too low (around 1.3) to realize the implied odds this hand relies on. Furthermore, Flash's 4-betting range is heavily weighted toward premium hands, and playing out of position post-flop in an inflated pot is highly unprofitable. The standard and optimal play is to fold our 3-bet bluff.

On the button with a small pocket pair (44), I want to open with a standard raise. Raising to 2.5x puts pressure on the blinds and gives me a chance to win the pot uncontested, while still playing a manageable pot if I get called, with the potential to flop a set.

K-2 offsuit is a very weak hand, especially when playing out of position from the Small Blind. Calling an open raise from the Button with such a dominated hand is a negative expected value play. I will fold and wait for a better spot to enter a pot.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 6s. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. While I am getting decent pot odds (150 to call into a 400 pot), 9-6 offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Pro has been active and aggressive, and I would likely have to hit the board hard to continue past the flop. As the current chip leader, I prefer to wait for better spots rather than defending with marginal garbage hands against a competent opponent who has position on me.

Queen-Four offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, even in the button position. My stack is sufficient, but I need to remain disciplined to avoid bleeding chips against aggressive opponents. Folding is the standard and correct play for this hand.

I am in the Small Blind with KQs, a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Flashlight has already folded from the button. The action is on me. I should raise here to build a pot and put pressure on Pro in the Big Blind, or potentially take the blinds down immediately. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate given the 50/100 blind structure and the current stack depths.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Deuces against a Small Blind open. We have a relatively deep stack compared to the bet size, giving me good implied odds to set-mine. Furthermore, I will have positional advantage post-flop. Calling to see if I hit a set is the most profitable play here.

I hit second pair (Queens) on the flop with a strong kicker (King). Having been the pre-flop aggressor, I'll lead out with a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot. This allows me to represent the Ace, protect my hand against potential draws, and find out where I stand in the hand.

The flop is A-Q-7 with two diamonds. I completely missed my set and am holding bottom pair with no meaningful draws. Flash's continuation bet on this board texture represents a strong range that often hits at least a pair of Queens or Aces. Since my primary goal was to set-mine and I failed to improve, the standard and most profitable play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I have been dealt Pocket Queens (Qh Qc) on the button, which is a premier starting hand in three-handed play. My goal is to build a significant pot while the blinds are relatively shallow compared to my stack. Both 'Pro' and 'Flashlight' have around 70BBs. I will open with a standard raise to 300 chips to induce action or take the blinds, keeping my range balanced but clearly leaning towards value here.

Holding pocket Kings in the Small Blind facing a Button open, a 3-bet is mandatory for value and to seize the initiative. Sizing to around 1100 (nearly 3.5x to 4x) works well out of position to either isolate the Button or take down the pot pre-flop.

Facing a raise from the button and a 3-bet from the small blind, my hand (A2 offsuit) is significantly dominated. The risk of being out of position against two aggressive ranges is too high, and the stack-to-blind ratio does not justify a speculative call. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision.

I have Pocket Queens, the third-best starting hand. After my initial raise, Pro 3-bet from the Small Blind. Given the 3-handed format and Pro's stack size of approximately 60BB, I will 4-bet to 2600 for value. This allows me to capitalize on my hand strength immediately, potentially inducing a shove from weaker hands like Jack-Jack, Ten-Ten, or Ace-King, while also protecting my equity against overcards.

With pocket Kings (Ks Kh) facing a 4-bet from the aggressive Button, we have a premium hand and a mandatory 5-bet all-in shove. We want to get all our chips in pre-flop to maximize value against his 4-betting range, which includes QQ, JJ, AK, and occasional bluffs. Calling would leave an awkward stack-to-pot ratio out of position and give our opponent a chance to outplay us post-flop.

I hold Pocket Queens in a three-handed game, which is an extremely strong hand. After my 4-bet to 2600, Pro has gone all-in for 7150 chips. To call, I need to commit an additional 4550 chips. The total pot after I call will be approximately 14400 chips. I need roughly 31.6% equity to justify the call. Against a range of JJ+, AK, QQ has about 47% equity. Even against a tighter range of QQ+, AK, I have enough equity given the price. Folding here would be a significant mistake in a short-handed high-stakes environment.

I am dealt 5s 2c on the button, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite having positional advantage, this hand has virtually no playability post-flop and extremely poor equity. Attempting to steal the blinds with such a weak holding is an unnecessary risk, especially given my comfortable chip lead. The fundamentally sound and optimal play is to simply fold.

I am in the small blind with a suited King. Since the button folded, I am facing only the big blind. Calling the 50 chips is a standard, low-risk move to see a flop in this position with a playable hand.

Flashlight limped from the small blind, and I am in the big blind with 5c 4c. While these are decent suited connectors, I prefer to check and see a flop for free rather than raising and potentially facing a re-raise from a trapping range or bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

The board is paired with two Queens, which is a very dry and static texture. I hold King-high with no significant draws. Since I am in the small blind and checked my option, checking the flop is the standard play to evaluate the opponent's strength while keeping the pot small, as I have little equity to bet for value or as a semi-bluff against a range that likely contains a Queen or a pocket pair.

On a paired board of Qd Qc 7d, I have 5c 4c with no direct connectivity or pair. Flashlight limped in the small blind and checked the flop. There is no reason to bluff here as I have zero equity if called, and checking back allows me to see a turn card for free in a small pot.

I am in the small blind holding King-high on a board of Qd-Qc-7d-Th. I have no made hand and no draws. Both players checked the flop, indicating a lack of strength. Checking again is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and see the river without committing more chips with a weak holding.

I am in the big blind with 5c 4c on a board of Qd Qc 7d Th. After checking pre-flop and on the flop, Flashlight has checked again on the turn. I currently have no pair and no draw. While the board is paired, Flashlight's passive line (limping SB and checking twice) suggests a very weak hand. However, I have zero showdown value. Despite this, since the pot is very small (200 chips) and I have no equity, there is little incentive to bluff into a range that might contain a weak 7 or a 10 that won't fold to a single bet. I will check back to see a free river card and re-evaluate.

I am holding King-high on a board of Qd-Qc-7d-Th-8h. Both the flop and turn were checked through, indicating that neither player has significant strength, but also that I have very little showdown value. Since I am first to act on the river and have already checked down the previous streets, checking is the most logical move to minimize losses. Bluffing into a board where a Queen or a pair is likely to be held by the opponent is high-risk and unlikely to succeed, so I will take a free showdown.

The board is Qd Qc 7d Th 8h and I hold 5c 4c. This is a limped pot from the Small Blind (Flashlight). The action has gone check-check on every street. I have 5-high, which has virtually no showdown value, as even most of the opponent's 'junk' hands (like 6-high or better) will beat me. However, in a pot this small (200 chips), bluffing is rarely worth the risk-to-reward ratio, especially since the opponent may feel priced in to call with any Ace-high, King-high, or weak pair they might have checked. Since I have no blockers to a pair and the pot is negligible, I will check and concede the hand.

I am on the button with Ace-high (Ah 5d). This is a strong position to open the pot and attempt to steal the blinds. A raise to 300 chips is a standard opening size that gives me a good chance to take down the pot immediately or see a flop with initiative.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s6s. Flashlight has opened to 300 from the Button. While 96s is a decent suited hand, playing it out of position against a 3x raise is generally unprofitable, especially with the Big Blind (Pro) yet to act, who has a large stack and could easily squeeze or put me in a difficult spot post-flop. Given the stack depths and the fact that I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand, I will elect to fold this speculative hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 2c 3s, which is one of the worst possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a standard 3x raise from the button, there is absolutely no reason to defend or get involved. An easy fold.

I am on the button with 9-6 offsuit. While 3-handed play requires a wider opening range from the button, 9-6 offsuit is significantly below the threshold of hands that are profitable to play, especially with an aggressive big stack (Pro) in the small blind. Given that Pro recently won a massive pot against me in hand #102, I should be cautious about entering pots with marginal hands until I have a better sense of how the dynamic has shifted. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.
KQo is a premium hand in a blind vs blind scenario. Raising to 3x puts pressure on the big blind, gives me the initiative, and frequently wins the pot outright or builds a good pot when called.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (2s, Th). My opponent, Pro, is an aggressive player and has raised from the Small Blind. Given the lack of playability and equity of my hand, calling is negative expected value. I should fold and wait for a better spot.

King-Five suited is a solid hand to open from the Button. Raising here allows me to steal the blinds or take the initiative post-flop against one of the blinds with a hand that can make strong flushes and top pairs.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit, which is a solid hand in a shorthanded game. Pro has opened from the button. Calling from the Small Blind puts me out of position, and with the Big Blind yet to act, a flat call could invite a squeeze play. A 3-bet serves to isolate the button and take control of the hand, potentially winning it pre-flop or allowing me to define the ranges early.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, a premium hand in three-handed play. The Button (Pro) opened for a standard raise, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) 3-bet to 900. Flashlight has been aggressive, but this 3-bet sizing is significant. By 4-betting to 2200, I exert maximum pressure on both opponents. It likely forces a fold from the original raiser (Pro) and puts Flashlight in a difficult position where they must either fold or commit a large portion of their 6175 stack. Given the strength of my hand and the need to protect my equity, a 4-bet is the most effective play to take down the pot pre-flop or isolate against a weaker range.

I opened with King-Five suited from the Button to try and steal the blinds, but I ran into a 3-bet from the Small Blind and a cold 4-bet from the Big Blind. This indicates extreme strength from at least one, if not both, opponents. My hand is completely unplayable against this level of aggression and range strength. Folding is the only viable option to preserve my stack.

The opponent (Flash) has cold 4-bet from the Big Blind after I 3-bet from the Small Blind. A cold 4-bet from the BB is a very strong move, typically representing a range of premium hands like QQ+, AK. Ace-Nine offsuit is not strong enough to call this 4-bet, especially given that I will be playing out of position post-flop with a relatively small remaining stack. It is best to fold, cut my losses, and preserve my stack for a more favorable spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a strong hand for this position in a three-handed game. I want to build a pot and potentially take down the blinds pre-flop. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to open the action.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd 8d. Flashlight has opened for 3x from the Button. While Q8s is a decent suited hand, calling from the Small Blind is generally sub-optimal because it allows the Big Blind (Pro), who is the current chip leader, a great opportunity to squeeze or see a cheap flop with positional advantage over me for the rest of the hand. Given that I would be out of position for the entire hand against a capable opponent on the button and potentially the chip leader in the Big Blind, I will choose to fold this marginal hand to preserve my stack for better opportunities where I have the initiative or position.
I am in the Big Blind facing a standard 3x raise from the Button. My hand, 94s, is far too weak to defend even against a loose opening range. Folding is the optimal play to preserve my stack.

I have Ace-Ten offsuit on the button, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. I will put in a standard open raise of 3x the big blind to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately or play the hand in position.

I am in the Small Blind with an unsuited, disconnected hand (9c 6h). Flash has raised to 3x the big blind from the Button. This hand has very poor playability and equity, especially out of position. The correct and standard decision is to fold.

I am in the big blind with King-Seven offsuit. The button has raised to 300, and I need to call 200 more to win a total pot of 450. Given the pot odds, defending the big blind is the standard play here, even with a mediocre hand like K7o. Folding would be too conservative given the favorable price to see a flop.

I am in the big blind with a weak pair of 7s on an Ace-high board. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small and see how the button (the pre-flop raiser) reacts to the board. Leading out would likely be ill-advised as the board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor significantly.

On a flop of Ah-Jc-7h, I have top pair with an Ace and a Ten kicker. This is a strong hand heads-up against the Big Blind. Flashlight has checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. I should bet for value, as I am likely ahead of most of Flashlight's calling range (middle pairs, draws, or weaker Aces). A bet of approximately half the pot is standard to build the pot and charge drawing hands.

The board is Ace-high and fairly connected. My pair of 7s is weak and unlikely to be ahead of the button's range, which significantly benefits from an Ace-high flop. Despite having the King of Hearts, I have very little equity to improve against a continuation bet. Continuing in this hand would be a negative expected value play, so I will fold to preserve my stack.
4h 5h is a suited connector, which plays well in position. From the Button, I can open-raise to steal the blinds or play a post-flop pot with the initiative. I will raise to 250.

I am in the Small Blind with As6s against a Button open of 250. My hand is well-suited for calling in this position as it has good playability and hidden equity. Given my stack size of 5975, calling is safer than a 3-bet, which could lead to being forced into a high-variance spot if the button plays back aggressively. I will call to see a flop and evaluate based on how the board texture connects with my hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Diamonds and Deuce of Clubs. Pro raised from the Button, and Flashlight called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call the remaining 150 chips, T2o is a very weak hand with extremely poor playability, especially out of position in a multi-way pot. Calling here would often lead to difficult post-flop situations where I am either dominated or forced to fold to any aggression. Therefore, I will make the disciplined fold and wait for a better spot.

The flop is dry and rainbow, providing very little help to my Ace-high hand with only backdoor flush potential. As the out-of-position player in the small blind against the button, checking is the standard play to see if my opponent makes a continuation bet. I do not have a strong enough hand to lead out, and checking keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to evaluate Pro's strength.

The board of Kh-7s-2d is extremely dry and heavily favors my pre-flop open-raising range, as I can easily have strong Kings (AK, KQ) while the Small Blind's flat-calling range is often capped and filled with missed broadway cards or middle pairs. With 4h 5h, I have almost zero showdown value but do have backdoor straight and flush potential. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot is highly effective here to fold out his missed overcards and weaker pairs, capitalizing on my perceived range advantage.

The opponent has made a very small bet (200 into a 800 pot), which gives me excellent pot odds (5:1) to call with my Ace-high and backdoor flush draw. While my hand currently lacks showdown value, the small bet sizing suggests the opponent might be probing or continuation betting with a marginal range. Calling allows me to keep the pot small and re-evaluate on the turn, especially since I have the Ace of Spades which provides some equity against ranges that might be semi-bluffing or testing the board.

I am in the small blind with Ace-high (As6s) on a dry board of Kh-7s-2d-9c. My opponent (Pro, on the button) has a range that strongly connects with this board, particularly containing Kings and high-card hands. Since I am first to act on the turn and the pot is small (1000), there is no value in betting my Ace-high as a bluff against a range that likely has me beat. Checking is the standard play to maintain pot control and realize equity if I improve or if it goes to showdown cheaply. I will check to see the river.

With 4h 5h on a Kh-7s-2d-9c board, I have absolutely no equity and no viable draws. Flashlight's call on the flop indicates he has some piece of the board, such as a weak King, a middle pair (like 88-JJ), a 7, or a float that may have picked up equity on the turn. Continuing to bluff here with zero equity is highly risky because I'm drawing almost dead if called. Checking to give up and save my chips for a more profitable spot is the most prudent play.

The board is Kh-7s-2d-9c-9h. My hand is As-6s (Ace-high). I am out of position and first to act. The opponent checked behind on the turn, suggesting they likely have a marginal hand or a missed draw, but they are also unlikely to fold any pair (a 7, 9, King, or pocket pair) to a bluff on this river. Betting into a checked-back range is high-variance and generally unprofitable here. Checking is the standard play to potentially reach a showdown with Ace-high, or to fold if they decide to value bet.

With 4h 5h, I have 5-high and absolutely zero showdown value. After my continuation bet was called on the flop and the turn checked through, Flashlight's range is heavily weighted towards marginal bluff-catchers like a 7, a small pocket pair (e.g., 88, TT), an Ace-high float, or a weak King. My check on the turn makes it difficult to represent a strong King credibly on the river, making a bluff here highly suspect. Attempting to steal the pot now is unnecessarily risky, as he will likely look us up with most of his bluff-catching range. It is best to check, concede the pot, and preserve my stack.

I am holding 4-2 offsuit on the button, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With no action before me, there is no reason to commit chips to the pot with such a poor hand. Folding is the most disciplined and strategically sound decision to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with K8o. After the button folds, it is down to me and the Big Blind (Pro). K8o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a blind-vs-blind situation to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

I am in the Big Blind with Q6o facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind. Q6o is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly post-flop and is often dominated. Folding is the standard and correct decision here to conserve chips.

I am on the button with 53 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand and does not justify an open-raise or a call. To maximize my stack over the remaining hands, I should play conservatively with garbage hands and wait for better spots, especially since I have a healthy stack after winning several recent pots.
With Q7o in the small blind, the hand is too weak to play profitably out of position against the big blind. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

QJs is a strong suited broadway hand. From the Button, this is a clear open. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) to build a pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately, while having good playability post-flop if called.

Holding Ace-Nine suited in the small blind against a button open, I want to take initiative and prevent the big blind from squeezing. 3-betting is a strong play here to isolate the button and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Clubs and Five of Spades (T5o). The Button (Pro) raised to 250 and the Small Blind (Flashlight) three-bet to 850. This hand is significantly below the threshold for continuing in a three-bet pot, especially out of position. I have a healthy stack and do not need to take high-variance risks with marginal or weak holdings. Folding is the only viable option here.

Flashlight has been aggressive with 3-bets from the blinds against my button opens. Facing a 3-bet to 850, Queen-Jack suited is a very strong and playable hand in position. It is slightly too weak to 4-bet for value and plays too well post-flop to turn into a 4-bet bluff or fold. Calling to see a flop in position with a manageable SPR is the most profitable choice.

I have top pair with a decent kicker on a relatively connected board. The board is wet with potential flush and straight draws, so checking would be passive and allow the opponent to realize equity for free. A standard continuation bet of half the pot is appropriate here to protect my hand against draws and extract value from worse hands like smaller pairs or float attempts.

The flop of 6h 9h 7s completely misses my Jc Qc. I have no straight or flush draws, and my overcards might not even be live against Flashlight's 3-betting range, which likely contains overpairs (TT-AA) or better high cards (AK, AQ). Facing a half-pot continuation bet with basically no equity or strong backdoor draws, folding is the only profitable play.

73 offsuit is a very weak starting hand with low equity. Despite being on the button, there is no strategic reason to invest chips with such a poor hand when I can simply fold and wait for a better spot to steal the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 4h, a very weak hand. Since the Button has folded, it is just me and the Big Blind (Pro). While it only costs 50 more to see a flop, 54o plays poorly out of position and is easily dominated. Given that 'Pro' has shown significant aggression and competence in previous hands, I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot rather than engaging in a potentially difficult post-flop situation with a sub-par hand.

I am on the button with Ten-Eight offsuit. In a three-handed game, the button opening range is typically wide, but Ten-Eight offsuit is right at the bottom of that range. Given that 'Pro' in the small blind has shown a high frequency of 3-betting and aggressive post-flop play, and 'Flashlight' in the big blind is also quite active, I will choose to fold this hand. Opening a marginal hand like T8o into two aggressive opponents increases the likelihood of being forced to play a bloated pot out of position or facing a 3-bet I cannot profitably call.

Jack-Deuce offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand. Even in a blind-versus-blind scenario with the action folded to me, it lacks high card strength, suitedness, and connectivity. Raising as a steal or limping is unprofitable in the long run with a hand this disconnected and weak. Folding to preserve my chip lead is the best decision.
Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak hand with no straight or flush potential. Since I am first to act on the Button and have no chips invested, folding is the optimal and standard play.

I am in the small blind with 3c 5h after the button (Pro) has folded. This is a very weak, uncoordinated hand, and playing it out of position against the big blind (Flash) is not a profitable long-term strategy. The risk of getting trapped in a weak spot post-flop outweighs the small cost to see a flop. Folding is the most disciplined decision.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of deuces. This is a strong starting hand in position, and given the table's frequent opening size of 300, a raise here is standard to attempt to steal the blinds or build a pot with a playable hand.

I am holding 7-2 suited in the small blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker. The button has opened for a raise to 300. Defending the small blind with such a poor hand out of position is a losing play in the long run, especially with a solid player in the big blind yet to act. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a 3bb open from the Button, A7o is a marginal hand to play out of position from the Big Blind. While we might have the raw equity to call, our realization will be poor due to reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage. Folding is the most standard and solid play here.

I am on the button with 63o, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have positional advantage, 63o is not strong enough to merit an open-raise or a call, especially with both opponents holding significant stacks. I will fold and wait for a more profitable hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 54o, a very weak unsuited connector. Flash folded on the Button. Playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind is unprofitable. I will fold and save my chips for better opportunities.
K3s is a standard open from the Button in a 3-handed game. Opening to 2.5x (250 chips) puts pressure on the blinds to defend while keeping our opening range appropriately wide and balanced.

I am in the Small Blind with a suited hand (Qc-9c) against a button raise. The pot odds (calling 200 to contest a pot that will be at least 600) justify a call. I have reasonable equity to see a flop and maintain control of my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 4c. Pro (Button) raised to 250, and Flashlight (Small Blind) called. While I am getting favorable pot odds of 4-to-1 to close the action, 9-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand with very poor post-flop playability. I would be out of position against two players for the remainder of the hand. Given that I have a healthy stack and the tournament is approaching its final stages, there is no need to commit chips with such a low-equity hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have top pair with a solid queen, but my kicker is relatively weak. The board is dry and disconnected, making it unlikely for my opponent to have improved significantly unless they hold a very strong hand. As I am out of position, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the most prudent play to control the pot size and observe their betting tendencies on this flop before committing more chips.

With Kc3c on a dry 5h Qd 2c board, I have some backdoor equity (clubs and straight potential) along with an overcard. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot is an effective way to fold out the opponent's overcards and weak hands, leveraging my range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor.

I hold top pair with a Queen. The board is dry, and the opponent has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a 550 pot. This bet size suggests a probing bet rather than a made monster hand. Folding top pair here is too tight, and raising would inflate the pot against a potentially better Queen or a trap. Calling is the most balanced approach to see how the hand develops on the turn while keeping the pot manageable out of position.

The board has paired on the turn (5h-Qd-2c-5c). I am holding top pair (Qc-9c). The turn went check-check after the flop action. Since it is checked to me on the turn, and I have top pair on a board that is not very dangerous, I should check again. There is no reason to bet into a player who checked behind on the turn, as I want to reach the showdown as cheaply as possible while keeping the pot controlled. Checking is the most standard line here to proceed to the river.

The 5c on the turn gives me a strong King-high flush draw. This is an excellent spot to double barrel as a semi-bluff. A bet will fold out better high-card hands like Ace-high, as well as small pocket pairs that peeled the flop. If called, I still have significant equity with 9 flush outs and potentially 3 King outs. A bet sizing of around 60% of the pot applies the necessary pressure.

I have top pair with a Queen (Qc-9c) on a board of 5h-Qd-2c-5c. Pro (Button) bet 600 into a 1000 pot on the turn. While the pairing of the 5 on the turn gives potential trips or full house possibilities to an opponent, it is equally likely to be a value bet with a better Queen, an overpair, or a continuation with a hand like JJ or TT. Given the pot odds of roughly 2.6:1, calling is statistically justified. My top pair is too strong to fold at this point, and raising would likely only isolate me against hands that have me crushed or force out bluffs. I will call to evaluate the river.

The river card (Jc) completed my flush (Qc-Jc-9c-5c-2c). Given that the pot is 2200 and I have a strong made hand, I should look to extract value. While there is a slight possibility of a better flush (if the opponent has the Ac or Kc of clubs) or a full house (with 55, QQ, or JJ), the board texture does not strongly suggest those hands given the previous action. Betting half the pot (1100) is a standard value bet size that should be called by hands like trips or two pair, which the opponent may have.

We completed our King-high flush on the river, but the board is paired (5s) and the Ace of clubs is still unaccounted for. Our opponent's half-pot lead could be a value bet with a worse flush, trip 5s, or a bluff, but it could also easily be the nut flush or a full house. Raising here is too thin, as it will likely only get called by hands that beat us (like the Ace-high flush or full houses) while folding out his bluffs and weaker value hands. Calling is the most balanced and profitable play, allowing us to capture value from his bluffs and worse hands while pot-controlling against the absolute nuts.

I am on the button with a very weak hand, Queen-Deuce offsuit. Given the limited depth of my stack (5625) and the fact that I am playing against two opponents, this hand does not have enough equity to justify an open-raise or a limp. The most disciplined and strategic play in this situation is to fold and wait for a more favorable hand to engage in the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 2h, which is a very weak hand. The action has folded to me. While I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, this hand plays poorly post-flop and Pro in the Big Blind has been active and has a significant chip lead. There is no reason to invest chips with such a marginal hand from out of position.

I am on the button with Q3o. While it is a 3-handed game, Q3o is a very weak hand that doesn't play well post-flop. Both Pro (SB) and Flashlight (BB) have shown willingness to defend and 3-bet frequently. There is no need to risk chips with a sub-par hand at this stage of the tournament when I can wait for a better opening or a more favorable spot to use my stack. I will fold.

KTo is a strong starting hand when folded to in the small blind. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300) to apply pressure to the big blind and aim to take down the pot or build it with a solid holding.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Ten offsuit. Against an open from the small blind, this is a very strong hand that warrants defending. Since the button folded and the small blind raised to 300, I need to call 200 more to see the flop. My hand is strong enough to play post-flop, so I will call.

On an A-7-7 flop, my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser is strong, as it contains many Aces and premium pocket pairs. Although I completely missed the flop and only have King-high, a small continuation bet of 1/3 pot will often fold out my opponent's unmade hands, underpairs, and random floats. If he calls or raises, I can easily give up, minimizing my losses.

I have top pair (Ace) with a strong Ten kicker on an Ace-Seven-Seven board. The board is relatively dry, and I currently hold the best hand against most of Pro's range, especially since I block other Ace-X combinations. Pro's small bet of 200 into a 600 pot is likely a continuation bet. Calling is the most prudent play to keep his bluffs in the pot and see what he does on the turn, as raising might fold out the hands I am currently dominating.

The turn brings a third 7 to the board, making it A-7-7-7. My King kicker plays, giving me trip 7s with an Ace-King kicker. However, my opponent called a continuation bet on an Ace-high flop, meaning his range is heavily weighted toward Aces (which now make a full house) or 7s (which make quads), as well as some flush draws and pocket pairs. There is no value in betting here, as worse hands will fold and I will only get called or raised by hands that crush me. I will check to exercise pot control, hoping to take this to a cheap showdown and potentially catch bluffs from missed draws.

The board shows three sevens (7c, 7h, 7d) and an Ace (Ah). With my holding of Ace-Ten, I have made a Full House (Aces full of Sevens). This is a very strong hand, but it is technically vulnerable if my opponent holds the final remaining seven (7s) for Four-of-a-Kind. Pro checked the turn, which could be an attempt to trap or a sign of weakness. Given the strength of my hand and the potential for a monster hand against me, checking is the most disciplined and balanced play, keeping the pot manageable while allowing me to reassess on the river.

The river brings a Queen, making the board 7-A-7-7-Q. My King plays as the kicker to the trip sevens and Ace, giving me 7-7-7-A-K. This is essentially the best possible non-full-house hand. However, betting here has no value since worse hands (like J-high or lower, which play the board's A-Q kickers) will simply fold, and I will only be called or raised by hands that beat me, such as Ax or Qx (which make full houses). Checking is the optimal play to realize my showdown value, keep the pot controlled, and potentially induce a bluff from busted flush draws.

I have Aces full of Sevens (Ac, Ah, 7c, 7h, 7d), which is an extremely strong hand. My opponent has checked both the turn and the river, which suggests they either have a mediocre hand they are trying to get to showdown with (like an Ace or a Queen) or they are pot-controlling. A bet of 500 into the 1000 pot is a solid value bet that should be called by hands I beat, while minimizing risk if they happen to have a Seven for Quads, which is unlikely given the board texture and action.

My hand is King-high, which plays as trip 7s with an Ace-King kicker (7-7-7-A-K) on this board. However, since the board is A-7-7-7-Q, any pocket pair, Ace, or Queen in my opponent's hand makes a full house and beats me. My opponent called a continuation bet on the A-7-7 flop, meaning their range is dense with Aces, pocket pairs, and flush draws. Their river bet is very consistent with a value bet from a full house. I only beat pure bluffs from missed flush draws, making this a clear fold.

Q6 offsuit is a weak hand even from the Button. It lacks connectivity, suitedness, and strong high card value. Given standard opening ranges and no immediate need to force the action since I hold a healthy chip lead, the best decision is to fold.

Pro (Button) folded, leaving me in a blind-versus-blind situation. With King-Ten suited, I have a strong hand that plays well heads-up. Raising allows me to take control of the hand, put pressure on the big blind, and potentially win the pot pre-flop or establish an initiative for post-flop play.

I am in the Big Blind with A8o. The Button has folded, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 3x. A8o is a strong hand in a blind-vs-blind battle, and I have position on the Small Blind for the rest of the hand. While a 3-bet is an option, calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my positional advantage without bloating the pot against a shorter stack. Flashlight's range in the SB is likely wide, and A8o performs well against that range.

I have flopped top pair with a King and a Ten kicker on a fairly dry board. As the pre-flop aggressor in this heads-up pot, a continuation bet is the standard play to maintain initiative, extract value from weaker hands like a lower King, middle pairs, or draw-heavy hands, and to protect my equity. Sizing the bet at 300 (half the pot) is appropriate for this texture.

In a blind-versus-blind scenario, Flashlight's range is wide. While the King on the board is a scare card, a continuation bet is standard here for almost his entire range. I have Ace-high with position and am getting 3:1 odds on a call. I will call one street to see if he slows down on the turn or if I improve my hand, utilizing my positional advantage to potentially win the pot later.

The turn card (Ace of Spades) is a very significant card in the Big Blind's calling range, as it hits many of the hands they would have called a pre-flop raise and a flop continuation bet with. My hand, King-Ten, is now likely second-best if my opponent holds any Ace. Betting into an opponent on an Ace-high board, especially when I only have a King, is risky and likely to result in me either betting into a better hand or folding out hands I could potentially beat or tie. Checking allows me to re-evaluate based on my opponent's action, pot control, and potentially see a free river card if they also check back.

On the turn, an Ace appears, which significantly improves my hand to top pair. Flashlight, the Small Blind, has checked after leading on a King-high flop. This check suggests they may be slowing down with a hand like a King or a draw, or perhaps they are pot-controlling. With an 8 kicker, my top pair is vulnerable to better Aces (like AJ or AT), but it is likely the best hand currently.
